coredata.dc:title: Double coating strategy simultaneously enhances emission, fluorescent thermal stability and water resistance of K2SiF6:Mn4+ phosphor
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yuelan
Wang, Lin
Wang, Tianman
Deng, Daishu
Lei, Jun
Qiang, Jiawei
Liao, Sen
Huang, Yingheng
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, K2SiF6:0.05Mn4+@GQDs 6 mg/mol@0.10K2SiF6 (the optimal sample, K2SiF6 = KSF, GQDs = NH3-contained graphene quantum dots) with excellent emission intensity, water resistance and thermal stability was synthesized by double coating strategy. The detail outcomes are as follows: (a) The emission intensity of phosphors are enhanced by the coating with GQDs and KSF. (b) The water resistance of the phosphors is slightly enhanced by coating with GQDs and greatly increased by coating with KSF due to the effective isolation of [MnF6]2- from water by the coating layer. (c) The NTQE (negative thermal quenching effect) of the samples is enhanced by the coating with GQDs and KSF. The strong NTQE mechanism is assigned to the increased electron-phonon interactions caused by the double coating. A WLED prototype with excellent performance (Ra = 88.4, CCT = 3725 K) at 20 mA drive current was assembled from the optimal sample, showing that it can be widely applications in WLED field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescent thermal stability
Water resistance
Double coating
Red emitting phosphor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21661006 and No. 21965004 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region , China (Grant No. 2019GXNSFDA245022 , No. 2020GXNSFAA159036 ), the Scientific Research Foundation of Guangxi University (Grant No. XDZ140116 ), the innovation Project of Guangxi Graduate Education (Grant No. YCSW2020015 ), the Students Experimental Skills and Innovation Ability Training Fund Project of Guangxi University (No. 202010593186 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns, FTIR, XPS and high resolution XPS of Mn 2p of the three samples, (i) KSF:0.05Mn4+,(ii) KSF:0.05Mn4+@GQDs 6 mg/mol,(iii) KSF:0.05Mn4+@GQDs 6 mg/mol@0.10KSF, (v) NH3-GQDs: (a) XRD patterns, (b) FTIR spectra, (c) XPS energy spectra, (d),(e),(f) High resolution XPS of Mn 2p.
SEM images and the corresponding EDS spectra of (i) KSF:0.05Mn4+,(ii) KSF:0.05Mn4+@GQDs 6 mg/mol,(iii) KSF:0.05Mn4+@GQDs 6 mg/mol@0.10KSF: (a), (b), (c) SEM images, (d), (e), (f) EDS spectra.

HRTEM image of samples (i-iii) and schematic diagram of the samples coating process ((i) KSF:0.05Mn4+, (ii) KSF:0.05Mn4+@GQDs, (iii) KSF:0.05Mn4+@GQDs@KSF): (a) (i), (b), (c) (ii), (d) (iii), (e) schematic diagram of the samples coating process.

Luminescent properties of samples (i)-(iv), (i) KSF:0.02Mn4+,(ii) KSF:0.05Mn4+@GQDs 6 mg/mol,(iii) KSF:0.05Mn4+@GQDs 6 mg/mol@0.10KSF and (iv) KSF@GQDs 4 mg/mol: (a), (b) Comparing PLE and PL spectra, (c) CIE chromaticity diagram of sample (iii), (d) decay curves.

PL spectra of samples (ia-iiia): (ia) K2SiF6:xMn4+, (iia) K2SiF6:0.05Mn4+@GQDs y
mg/mol, (iiia) K2SiF6:0.05Mn4+@GQDs 6 mg/mol @zK2SiF6.

Water resistance test of three samples: (i)KSF:0.05Mn4+, (ii) KSF:0.05Mn4+@GQDs 6 mg/mol, (iii)KSF:0.05Mn4+@GQDs 6 mg/mol@0.10KSF: (a) PL integrated intensities with different soaking time in water, (b) photographs under natural light.

Thermal performances of samples (i)-(iii), (i) KSF:0.05Mn4+,(ii) KSF:0.05Mn4+@GQDs 6 mg/mol,(iii) KSF:0.05Mn4+@GQDs 6 mg/mol@0.10KSF: (a), (b), (c) PL spectra, (d) Relative intensity curves, (e) Integrated intensity curves of stoke and anti-stoke peaks, (f) Chromaticity shift of the sample (iii), (g) CIE color coordinates of the sample (iii), (h) E
a curves, (i) TG curve of the sample (iii).

Performances of prototype WLED, using the mixing phosphors of (iii)KSF:0.05Mn4+ @GQDs 6 mg/mol@0.10KSF and YAG:Ce3+ based on InGaN chip under a 20 mA drive current: (a) Electroluminescence spectra, (b) CIE chromaticity diagram and (c) Luminescence photo.
ICP element analysis results of samples (i-iii).

row.entry: Relative emission intensity and corresponding time

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Negative thermal quenching effect of some samples.

row.entry: Integrated emission intensity and corresponding temperature

head.ce:title: Double coating strategy simultaneously enhances emission, fluorescent thermal stability and water resistance of K2SiF6:Mn4+ phosphor

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, K2SiF6:0.05Mn4+@GQDs 6 mg/mol@0.10K2SiF6 (the optimal sample, K2SiF6 = KSF, GQDs = NH3-contained graphene quantum dots) with excellent emission intensity, water resistance and thermal stability was synthesized by double coating strategy. The detail outcomes are as follows: (a) The emission intensity of phosphors are enhanced by the coating with GQDs and KSF. (b) The water resistance of the phosphors is slightly enhanced by coating with GQDs and greatly increased by coating with KSF due to the effective isolation of [MnF6]2- from water by the coating layer. (c) The NTQE (negative thermal quenching effect) of the samples is enhanced by the coating with GQDs and KSF. The strong NTQE mechanism is assigned to the increased electron-phonon interactions caused by the double coating. A WLED prototype with excellent performance (Ra = 88.4, CCT = 3725 K) at 20 mA drive current was assembled from the optimal sample, showing that it can be widely applications in WLED field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: WLEDs have great prospects for applications in lighting displays and other fields because of their energy efficiency, long lifetime, small size, and planar package [1,2]. Mn4+ activated fluoride phosphors are an important component of WLEDs, which can enhance the color rendering index and reduce the associated color temperature [3–5].

In practice, WLEDs convert a portion of their electrical energy into heat during operation, resulting in WLEDs operating temperatures as high as 150 °C. The elevated temperature causes temperature thermal quenching, which affects the stability of the device's color coordinates and luminescent efficiency. Therefore, thermal stability is an important parameter for evaluating phosphor performance. In addition, Mn4+-activated fluoride red phosphors are highly susceptible to hydrolysis in humid environments, and Mn4+ can turn into manganese oxides or hydroxides, which can seriously damage the lifetime of WLEDs. Thus, it is an urgent task to improve the water resistance of fluoride phosphors [5].

Some Mn4+-activated phosphors with anomalous thermal quenching have been reported. Such findings have led to the enhancement of the luminescence thermal stability of the fluoride phosphors. For example, Fang et al. [6] prepared a novel Rb2SiF6:Mn4+ phosphor by co-precipitation method, and the relative luminescence intensity of the sample at 250 °C exceeded that at 25 °C. Wang et al. [7] synthesized a red phosphor Rb2TiF6:Mn4+ with efficient and stable luminescence properties. The luminescence intensity of Rb2TiF6:Mn4+ increased to 135% of that at room temperature and reached a maximum when the test temperature was increased to 120 °C. ten Kate et al. [8]. coated the surface of K2SiF6:Mn4+ with Al2O3, which at 200 °C, it achieved a luminescence intensity of 104.6% of that at room temperature. In our previous work [9], negative thermal quenching was also observed for K2SiF6:Mn4+ after coating with GQDs.

Mn4+-activated fluoride red phosphors are poorly resistant to water. At present, the coating is mainly applied on the phosphor surface, and the coating layer can prevent the hydrolysis of the [MnF6]2- group. At this stage, the coating layer materials can be divided into organic and inorganic coing materials. For example, Zhou et al. [10] used octadecyltrimethoxysilane (ODTMS) for superhydrophobic surface modification of K2TiF6:Mn4+. Arunkumar et al. [11] formed a hydrophobic protective layer on K2SiF6:Mn4+ using the organic oleic acid. Jang et al. [12] used cetyltrimethylsilane for hydrophobic modification of the K2SiF6:Mn4+ phosphor surface. All of the above methods can strengthen the water resistance of Mn4+-activated fluoride phosphors, but the organic shell layer is unstable at high temperatures or in contact with organic solvents. Therefore, researchers have proposed reverse cation exchange strategies or surface deposition strategies to form K2TiF6 or K2SiF6 matrix shell layers on surfaces of the K2TiF6:Mn4+ or K2SiF6:Mn4+ to block the reaction of water molecules with [MnF6]2-. There are also methods to improve the water resistance by adjusting the crystal structure of the matrix material, Fang et al. [13] synthesized K0.070Ba0.965TiF6:Mn4+ using K+ instead of Ba2+, and the relative luminescence intensity was about 65% that of the initial luminescence intensity after immersion 150 min. Lang et al. [14] synthesized K2Ge0.7Si0.3F6:Mn4+ by replacing Ge4+ with Si4+ after immersion 168 h in water, and the relative photoluminescence intensity remained at 78% that of the initial value.

The above methods can improve the thermal stability or water resistance of fluoride red phosphors to some extent. However, in the practical application of WLEDs, the problems caused by unstable performance of red emitting phosphors in humidity and high temperature have not to be solved simultaneously. Therefore, it is a challenging task to enhance the luminous intensity, water resistance and fluorescence thermal stability of phosphors at the same time.

In this experiment, a series of K2SiF6:Mn4+@GQDs@K2SiF6 phosphors were prepared by a double coating strategy. Coating with GQDs improves the luminous intensity and fluorescent thermal stability of K2SiF6:Mn4+. A Coating of K2SiF6 makes K2SiF6:Mn4+ have excellent water resistance. The problems of Mn4+-doped red emitting phosphors application in high temperature and humid environment are successfully solved. The synthesis strategy of the novel core-double-shell structure phosphor broadens the application scope of phosphors and has great potential for practical applications.
The experimental section is depicted in the Supplementary Material. Information about the properties of NH3-GQDs is also in the Supplementary Material.

Table 1 shows the results of ICP elemental analysis of three samples with the following molecular formulae: (i) KSF:0.05Mn4+, (ii) KSF:0.05Mn4+@GQDs 6 mg/mol and (iii) KSF:0.05Mn4+@GQDs 6 mg/mol@0.10KSF. The outcomes indicate that surface of the sample (iii) is probably covered by K2SiF6.

The FTIR analysis allows to determine the changes in the functional groups on the phosphor surface after coating with GQDs. As shown in Fig. 1b, GQDs have an intense absorption peak near 1630 cm−1, which is a characteristic peak of CC or CO stretching vibration. Fig. 1b shows that both the sample (i) and the sample (ii) have absorption peaks near 1630 cm−1, but the latter one is stronger than the former one. The absorption peak of CO stretching vibration shown in the sample (i) may be due to the adsorption of carbon dioxide in air, while the absorption peak shown in sample (ii) may be an overlapping peak of the CO vibration of carbon dioxide and the CC vibration of GQDs, suggesting that GQDs can be effectively coated on the KSF:0.05Mn4+ surface by hydrothermal method, which is supported by our previous work [9].

The XPS characterization of the samples (i-iii) was carried out at room temperature to analyze the chemical composition of their surfaces. As shown in Fig. 1c, the characteristic signal peaks of K 2s, K 2p, Si 2s, Si 2p, F 1s, Mn 2p, O 1s, and C 1s are all present in the full spectra of the samples. Among them, the C 1s and O 1s signals are attributed to GQDs, CO2, and H2O. High-resolution XPS patterns of Mn 2p in the samples are shown in Fig. 1d–f. As shown in the plots, the Mn 2p signal on the surface of sample (ii) is lower than that on the surface of sample (i), which may be attributed to the blocking of the Mn 2p signal by the GQDs on the surface of sample (ii). Meanwhile, almost no Mn 2p signal is observed on the surface of sample (iii), which indicates that the K2SiF6 layer is indeed formed. The Si 2p content on the surface of samples (i-iii) was calculated from the XPS and shown in Table S2. As can be seen from the table, the atomic percentages of Si 2p on the surface of the samples do show: (i) high, (ii) low and (iii) high, which are agreement with the results of ICP. This proves that K2SiF6:Mn4+ is most likely to be coating by the KSF layer.

The morphologies of samples (i-iii) were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Fig. 2 a–c show that the samples (i-iii) all have irregular morphologies with diameters ranging from 1 to 20 μm. After coating with GQD, sample (ii) aggregates into smooth irregular bulk particles. After the coating with K2SiF6, the sample (iii) grows into lamellar lumpy particles which the surface is attached with fine particles. The above results show that the coating with GQDs and K2SiF6 has a great influence on the morphology and size of the synthesized samples. The EDS spectra of Fig. 2d–f show the elemental composition of the samples (i-iii), where K, Si, Mn and F signals are detected in all three phosphors, but C signal is detected in sample (ii). It is obvious that the C element of the sample (ii) is from GQDs, which further proves that GQDs are successfully coated on the phosphor surface. In summary, GQDs and KSF are sequentially coated on the surface of KSF:0.05Mn4+.

HRTEM is used to observe the microstructure of the samples, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3a is the HRTEM image of sample (i) KSF:0.05Mn4+, from which it can be seen that sample (i) shows smooth surface particles with relatively clear boundaries. Fig. 3b and c are HRTEM images of sample (ii) KSF:0.05Mn4+@GQDs, which shows that the surface of sample (ii) is coated with granular GQDs with a diameter of 4–5 nm. Fig. 3c clearly shows that there are lattice stripes of GQDs, indicating that GQDs are successfully coated on the phosphor surface. Fig. 3d is an HRTEM image of sample (iii) KSF:0.05Mn4+@GQDs@KSF, which shows a clear core-shell structure on the surface, with a gray spherical inner core of KSF:0.05Mn4+ phosphor and a black shell layer of K2SiF6 with a thickness of about 50 nm at the edge, indicating that GQDs and K2SiF6 are successfully wrapped on the surface of KSF:0.05Mn4+ phosphor surface, forming a core-double shell structure of KSF:0.05Mn4+@GQDs@KSF. Fig. 3e shows the schematic diagram of the cross-section of the formed double coated phosphor. Combining the results of XPS, FTIR, and HRTEM, it can be concluded that the K2SiF6:Mn4+ surface is sequentially coated with GQDs and K2SiF6 shell layers.

The excitation and emission spectra of the samples (i-iii) and the undoped Mn4+ sample (iv) K2SiF6@GQDs 6 mg/mol) are given in Fig. 4 a and b. All samples (i-iii) show two broadband excitation peaks at 362 and 467 nm, and they are owed to the spin-allowed transitions of 4A2g→4T1g and 4A2g→4T2g, respectively [16,17]. The red emission peak in the 550–680 nm region is attributed to the 2Eg→4A2g spin-forbidden transition of Mn4+ [18,19]. The sample (iv) does not emit light, under excitation of 467 nm.

GQDs and K2SiF6 coating had two effects on K2SiF6:0.05Mn4+: (a) The fluorescence emission intensity of the sample (ii) (coated with GQDs) was 41% higher than that of the sample (i) (uncoated with GQDs), which is similar to our previous report [9]. (b) The fluorescence emission intensity of the sample (iii) increased by 55% compared to the sample (i), indicating that the emission intensity is further enhanced by coating with K2SiF6. The reason for this phenomenon is that: (a) The conjugated larger π-bonds of GQDs can coordinate with Mn4+ to donate electron pairs for Mn4+ and enhance the luminescence of Mn4+. (b) The coating shell of K2SiF6 interrupts the energy delivery to the surface defects, so the luminescence of the phosphor is enhanced [2].

The color coordinates calculated from the emission spectra of the samples under 467 nm blue light excitation using color coordinate software are shown in Fig. 4c. The color coordinates of samples (i-iii) are marked in the figure and are located at (0.6888, 0.3111), (0.6873, 0.3125) and (0.6873, 0.3125) in that order. As can be seen from Fig. 4c, the color coordinates of the three samples are close to the standard value of NTSC (0.67,0.33), and the coordinate points almost overlap, indicating that the coating with GQDs and KSF has little effect on the color coordinates of phosphors.

Fig. 4d shows the fluorescence lifetime decay curves of samples (i-iii). The fluorescence lifetimes (τ) of the samples (i-iii) are 6.11, 6.18 and 6.26 ms, respectively. This suggests that the coating with GQDs and K2SiF6 layers can enhance the fluorescence lifetime.

The internal quantum yield (QYi) of the phosphors are calculated with Eq. (S1) [20]. The QYi values of samples (i-iii) were 73.66, 93.61 and 99.03%, respectively. The order of QYi values of the samples was QYi (i)≪ QYi (ii)<QYi (iii), indicating that the coating with GQDs caused a significant increase in QYi values, while the coating with K2SiF6 caused a further slight increase in QYi values.

For fluorescent materials, it is essential to optimize their luminescence properties. The emission spectra of the samples designed and synthesized by varying the addition of K2MnF6 and using different amounts of GQDs and K2SiF6 are given in Fig. 5 . The doping concentration of Mn4+ is a key factor affecting the fluorescence properties of fluoride phosphors. Fig. 5a shows the emission spectra of the samples (ia) with different x (x = molar ratio of K2MnF6/(K2MnF6 + K2SiF6)). Except for the fluorescence intensity, the position and shape of the K2SiF6:xMn4+ fluorescence spectrum basically do not change significantly with the change of Mn4+ concentration. As depicted in the illustrations of Fig. 5a, the fluorescence intensity of the phosphor rises with x and reaches a peak at x = 0.05, and then the fluorescence intensity gradually decreases as x increases. This is due to the fact that the non-radiation cross-relaxation between adjacent Mn4+ reduces the number of effective fluorescence centers, which leads to concentration quenching. To further optimize the luminescence performance of samples, K2SiF6:0.05Mn4+@GQDs y mg/mol (y = mass of NH3-GQD/mole of KSF:0.05Mn4+) were synthesized by hydrothermal method. The corresponding emission spectra of K2SiF6:0.05Mn4+@GQDs y mg/mol are given in Fig. 5b, except for the difference in luminescence intensity, their luminescence peak positions and peak shapes are the same, all have seven typical characteristic linear emission peaks. Therefore, y = 6 mg/mol was determined as the optimal coating concentration. The effects of different K2SiF6 coating concentrations (z = molar ratio of K2SiF6/K2SiF6:0.05Mn4+) on the luminescence performance of K2SiF6:0.05Mn4+@GQDs y mg/mol@zK2SiF6 are given in Fig. 5c. From Fig. 5c, it can be seen that the optimal coating concentration is z = 0.20 (Actual coverage is 0.10).

To test the water resistance of the samples (i-iii), we immersed them in water and monitored the luminescence intensity of the samples in real time, as shown in Fig. 6 . The PL integrated intensity of the phosphors immersed in water for different times are given in Fig. 6a. From Fig. 6a, it can be seen that: (a) When t = 30 min, the emission intensities of the samples (i-iii) are reduced to 50.87%, 85.16% and 95.68% of their initial ones, respectively. (b) When t = 480 min, the emission intensities of the samples (i-iii) are dropped to 10.30%, 37.98% and 95.45% of their initial values, respectively. (c) When t > 450 min, the luminescence intensity curves tend to level off and remains basically the same.

The results in Fig. 6a can be explained as follows: (a) In extremely humid environments, [MnF6]2- on the surface of sample hydrolyzes to produce dark brown MnO2 or Mn(OH)4 and the surface is covered with hydrolysis products of non-luminescent Mn, leading to the failure of luminescence [20]. (b) the curve (ii) shows water resistance improvement of the sample (ii) after coating with GQDs. This may be attributed to the large surface area of GQDs, which makes the [MnF6]2- on surface of the sample to reduce contacting with water. Therefore, the water resistance of the sample (ii) has been improved. (c) The sample (iii) with the protective layer of K2SiF6 can achieve the isolation of the inside Mn4+ from outside water molecules. Thus, the emission intensity of the sample (iii) can maintain 95.45% of the original one after 480 min immersion in water.

It has been reported that surface coating on samples or improving the crystal field environment of matrices are very effective ways to improve the water resistance of phosphors [21–27]. Cs2NbOF5:Mn4+ [21], OD-KTF [22], K2SiF6@SiO2 [23], Cs2TiF6:Mn4+@Cs2TiF6 [24], BaGeF6:Mn4+@PPG [25], P-KSF [26], and K2SiF6:Mn4+@CaF2 [27] are examples of applying the above strategies, and their water resistance performance compared with that of the samples (i-iii) are presented in Table 3 . In the comparison in Table 3, it is clear the sample (iii) has very good water resistance.

Fig. 6b and c visually gives the photographs of the three samples before and after 8 h of immersion. They are all orange-yellow powder before immersion, and the color of the samples (i-ii) changes from orange-yellow to brown-black after 8 h immersion, while sample (iii) still maintains its orange-yellow color. It can be clearly seen that the water resistance of the sample (iii) with double coating with GQDs and K2SiF6 is significantly improved.

The analyses for the thermal stability of samples (i-iii) are given in Fig. 7 . Fig. 7a–c show emission spectra of the samples excited at 467 nm under the variable temperatures, which the position and shape of the emission peaks do not change at different test temperatures. The sample's integrated emission intensities at 30–270 °C are given in Fig. 7d. Fig. 7d shows that the emission intensity increases with rising of temperature (30–210 °C), then the luminous intensities gradually decrease when the test temperature exceeds 210 °C. The curves have a parabolic shape, which suggests that samples (i-iii) have NTQE. However, the NTQE strength is different for the three samples, and the order of the strength is (iii) ≫ (ii) > (i). The NTQE was not reported by the works of K2SiF6:Mn4+@K2SiF6 [28,29] and K2TiF6:Mn4+@K2TiF6 [2]. The integrated emission intensities of the samples (i-iii) at 210 °C are 158%, 204% and 326% of their original ones at 30 °C, respectively, showing an increasing trend of the NTQE. It is seen from Fig. 7d that the NTQE is improved by coating with GQDs, then it is further significantly enhanced by coating with K2SiF6. So, the enhancement of the NTQE for the samples (ii-iii) can be explained as follows: (a) The conjugated large π-bonds of GQDs are coordnated to Mn4+ ions, and provide electron pairs to Mn4+ ions, therefore the NTQE is improved by coating with GQDs. (b) Coating layer of K2SiF6 interrupts the pathway of energy delivery to the surface defects, and results in a further significant enhancement of the NTQE.

The above phenomena of the NTQE can be described with Eqs. (S2, S3) (theory of electron-phonon interaction or phonon-induced theory) [30]. Eqs. (S2, S3) indicate that the mechanism of NTQE for the samples (i-iii) is that the phonon-induced radiative transition probability (RTP) increases faster than the non-radiative transition probability (NTP). In other words, in a certain temperature range, the electron-phonon interaction increases with increasing temperature. The equations clearly show that I
as and I
s increase with increasing temperature [31,41], and I
as rises faster than I
s , which is in agreement with the results in Fig. 7e. So, the NTQE mechanism of the three samples is mechanism of electron-phonon interaction.

In practice, WLEDs will convert part of the electrical energy into heat during operation, which will raise the operating temperature of WLEDs to about 150 °C. The increase in temperature affects the luminous intensity of the phosphor, thus affecting the stability of the device's color coordinates and lumen efficiency. Therefore, thermal stability is an important parameter for judging phosphor performance. Many studies have been conducted for obtaining red-emitting phosphors with good luminescence thermal stability [31–34].

It is shown that the NTQE could be used to promote the luminescence thermal stability of Mn4+ red phosphors. For example, K2SiF6:Mn4+@ GQDs [35], CsMoO2F3:Mn4+ [34], α-K3AlF6:Mn4+ [36], K2TiF6:Mn4+ [37], K2SiF6:xMn4+ [38], K2SiF6:Mn4+ [39] and CsNaGeF6:Mn4+ [40] are the phosphors that have the NTQE. The NTQE strengths of the above phosphors and the three samples are shown in Table 4 . From the comparison in Table 4, it is clear that the samples (ii-iii) have excellent fluorescence thermal stability.

The chromaticity change (ΔE) of the sample (iii) with temperature are computed using Eq. (S4) [42–45]. As shown in Fig. 7f, the curve is a nonlinear curve with ΔE gradually increasing with increasing temperature. The ΔE values of sample (iii) at 150, 175 and 200 °C were 17.85 × 10−3, 22.55 × 10−3, and 27.39 × 10−3, respectively. The ΔE at 175 °C for sample (iii) is better than the ΔE of CaAlSiN3:Eu2+ (36 × 10−3) [43], which further proves the excellent thermal stability of sample (iii).

CIE color coordinates of the sample (iii) at 30–270 °C are given in Fig. 7g. It is seen that between 30 and 150 °C, the color coordinates change just (0.007,-0.0066). It can be observed that the color coordinates of the phosphor changed only slightly with the rising of the temperature. The results indicate that sample (iii) has excellent color stability in the range of 30–270 °C and can satisfy the application of WLEDs.

Thermal quenching activation energy (Ea) of the phosphor is calculated by using Eqs. (S5-S6), and the results are shown in Fig. 7h, which Ea values of the samples (i-iii) are 1.41, 1.48 and 1.50 eV, respectively. The results show that the coating with GQDs can effectively increase the Ea of K2SiF6:Mn4+.

The TG curve of the sample (iii) was tested in the range of 40–950 °C with a temperature rise rate of 10 °C/min. As shown in Fig. 7i, the mass loss is just 0.302% before 200 °C, which may be owing to the removal of water adsorbed on the phosphor surface. Based on the factor that the LED chip only raises the temperature of the environment to 150 °C during operation, it is concluded that the sample (iii) is an excellent red emitting component for application in warm white LEDs.

To further verify the suitability of the prepared red phosphors for solid-state lighting. Prototype WLEDs are packaged using a combination of epoxy-coated blue light-emitting chip, yellow phosphor YAG:Ce3+ and sample (iii) and tested at a drive of 20 mA. Fig. 8 a is the electroluminescence spectrum of the corresponding WLEDs, and the CIE color coordinates of the WLEDs are obtained using its spectral data as (x = 0.4082, y = 0.3663) (Fig. 8b). Fig. 8c shows that the WLEDs emit bright warm white light with the following corresponding optoelectronic parameters, color temperature (CCT, Tc) = 3232 K, color rendering index R
a = 92.5, R
9 = 87.0, and luminescence efficiency (LE) = 102.3 lm W−1. The above packaging test results indicate that sample (iii) can be used to improve the color rendering performance of WLEDs and the water resistance of the devices.

In conclusion, KSF:0.05Mn4+@GQDs 6 mg/mol@0.10KSF red emitting phosphor with excellent emission intensity, water resistance and fluorescence thermal stability was synthesized through a combination of ion exchange method and hydrothermal coating method: (a) The PL intensity of the phosphors are enhanced through coating with GQDs and KSF. Thus, the emission intensities of KSF:0.05Mn4+@GQDs 6 mg/mol and KSF:0.05Mn4+@GQDs 6 mg/mol@0.10KSF were 41% and 55% higher than KSF: Mn4+, respectively. (b) The water resistance of the phosphor is slightly increased with the GQDs coating, whereas the water resistance is greatly improved with the KSF coating. The integrated emission intensity of the samples (i-iii) are decreased to 10.30%, 37.98% and 95.45% of their original ones for immersion in water for 480 min, respectively. (c) The NTQE of the samples were strengthened by the coating with GQDs and KSF, respectively. And the NTQE of the three samples is (iii) KSF:0.05Mn4+@GQDs 6 mg/mol@0.10KSF ≫ (ii) KSF:0.05Mn4+@GQDs 6 mg/mol > (i) KSF:0.05Mn4+, which their integrated emission intensities at 210 °C are 158%, 204% and 326% of their original ones at 30 °C, respectively. The causes of enhanced water resistance are assumed as that: (a) After coating the GQDs, the surface of the phosphor is covered with GQDs, reducing the contact of [MnF6]2- on the surface with water. (b) The double-layer coating with GQDs and KSF formed on the phosphor surface can effectively isolate [MnF6]2- from water molecules. In addition, mechanism of the strong NTQE is assigned to increasing the electron-phonon interactions induced by double coating with GQD and KSF.

By using the KSF:0.05Mn4+@GQDs 6 mg/mol@0.10KSF and YAG:Ce3+ as red emitting component and yellow emitting component, respectively, a prototype warm WLED based on a blue emitting chip (InGaN) with a luminous efficiency of 102.3 lm W−1, CCT value of 3232 K, and Ra value of 92.5 was packaged. The good performances of the WLED suggest that KSF:0.05Mn4+@GQDs 6 mg/mol@0.10KSF has wide application in the field of WLEDs.

Yuelan Li: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Lin Wang: Investigation. Tianman Wang: Investigation. Daishu Deng: Investigation. Jun Lei: Investigation. Jiawei Qiang: Investigation. Sen Liao: Conceptualization, Supervision. Yingheng Huang: review&editing, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal synthesis, morphology and photoluminescent properties of an Mn4+-doped novel red fluoride phosphor elpasolite K2LiAlF6
J. Mater. Chem. C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.W. Zhu, L. Huang, R. Zou, J.H. Zhang, J.B. Yu, M.M. Wu, J. Wang, Q. Su, Hydrothermal synthesis, morphology and photoluminescent properties of an Mn4+-doped novel red fluoride phosphor elpasolite K2LiAlF6, J. Mater. Chem. C 4 (2016) 5690−5695.
